articles.title: The Phone System Migration Nobody Talks About: Moving from One VoIP to Another
articles.body:
Most migration guides assume you are moving from a legacy PBX to VoIP for the first time. But what about the second migration — when your current VoIP provider is not cutting it and you need to switch to a different one?

This is actually harder than the first migration. Here is why, and how to do it without disrupting your business.

## Why VoIP-to-VoIP Is Harder

| Challenge | First Migration (PBX to VoIP) | Second Migration (VoIP to VoIP) |
|-----------|-----------------------------|---------------------------------|
| Number porting | From telco (straightforward) | From VoIP provider (may resist) |
| User expectations | Low (any improvement is welcome) | High (they expect at least the same features) |
| Integration rewiring | Fresh setup | Must replicate existing CRM/helpdesk connections |
| Training | Everyone learns together | People resent relearning |
| Political risk | Approved project | "Why are we changing again?" |

## The Porting Trap

Some VoIP providers make it intentionally difficult to port numbers away. Tactics I have seen:

- **30-day cancellation notice required** before they will release numbers
- **Porting department only available by email** with 5-day response times
- **"Your account has a balance"** — even $0.47 blocks the port
- **LOA rejection** on technicalities (wrong address format, missing middle initial)

Before you sign with any provider, test the exit process. Call their porting department. Ask: "If I wanted to port my numbers out, what is the process and timeline?" Time the response. If it takes more than 24 hours to get an answer, imagine how long the actual port will take.

## The Parallel Run Strategy

The safest approach:

| Week | Action |
|------|--------|
| Week 1 | Set up new provider with temporary numbers |
| Week 2 | Configure all features (IVR, ring groups, recording, CRM) |
| Week 3 | Pilot with 5-10 users on temporary numbers |
| Week 4 | Fix issues found during pilot |
| Week 5 | Submit port request to move numbers from old to new |
| Week 6-7 | Port executes (keep old system active as backup) |
| Week 8 | Decommission old provider |

The key insight: set up everything on temporary numbers FIRST. Test thoroughly. Only submit the port request after you are confident the new system works. This way, if something goes wrong, your old system is still running on your real numbers.

## Feature Parity Checklist

Before migrating, document every feature your team actually uses on the current system:

- [ ] Auto-attendant (record the exact menu structure)
- [ ] Ring groups (list every group and its members)
- [ ] Call recording settings (who is recorded, retention period)
- [ ] Voicemail greetings (save the audio files)
- [ ] Speed dials and BLF keys
- [ ] CRM integration mappings
- [ ] Call flow rules (business hours, holidays, after-hours)
- [ ] Fax configurations
- [ ] Conference bridge settings

Missing even one of these during migration will generate complaints.

## What I Recommend

Providers like [DialPhone](https://dialphone.com) handle VoIP-to-VoIP migrations specifically. They assign a migration specialist who documents your current setup, replicates it on their platform, and manages the porting process end-to-end. The parallel run period is included at no extra cost.

The second migration does not have to be painful. It just requires more planning than the first one.

articles.title: I Measured the Actual Productivity Impact of VoIP vs Landlines Over 12 Months
articles.body:
Everyone says VoIP improves productivity. Nobody shows the data. So I tracked it.

For 12 months, I measured productivity metrics at a 65-person insurance brokerage — 6 months before their VoIP migration and 6 months after. Same team, same workload, same management. The only variable was the phone system.

## What I Measured

| Metric | How I Measured It | Why It Matters |
|--------|------------------|----------------|
| Calls answered per agent per day | ACD reports | Direct productivity |
| Average handle time | CDR analysis | Efficiency |
| Missed call rate | PBX/VoIP reports | Customer experience |
| Time to return missed calls | Manual tracking by team leads | Responsiveness |
| CRM entries per agent per day | Salesforce reports | Documentation compliance |
| Employee satisfaction (phone system) | Monthly survey, 1-5 scale | Adoption indicator |

## The Results

### Call Volume and Handling

| Metric | Before (Landline) | After (VoIP) | Change |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|--------|
| Calls answered/agent/day | 34 | 41 | +20.6% |
| Average handle time | 4:12 | 3:38 | -13.5% |
| Missed call rate | 18.3% | 6.7% | -63.4% |
| Time to return missed calls | 2.4 hours | 22 minutes | -84.7% |
| After-hours calls handled | 0% | 31% (mobile app) | New capability |

### CRM and Documentation

| Metric | Before | After | Change |
|--------|--------|-------|--------|
| CRM entries per agent per day | 12 | 29 | +141.7% |
| Calls with CRM notes attached | 38% | 94% | +147.4% |
| Contact records with phone interaction history | 45% | 100% | +122.2% |

The CRM improvement was the biggest surprise. With automatic call logging, agents stopped manually entering call records. Every call — inbound, outbound, missed — was automatically logged with timestamp, duration, and recording link.

### Employee Satisfaction

| Month | Score (1-5) | Notes |
|-------|------------|-------|
| Month -6 (landline) | 2.8 | Baseline |
| Month -3 (landline) | 2.7 | Slight decline |
| Month 1 (VoIP) | 3.1 | Initial adjustment |
| Month 2 (VoIP) | 3.8 | Features discovered |
| Month 3 (VoIP) | 4.2 | Mobile app adoption |
| Month 6 (VoIP) | 4.4 | Stable high satisfaction |

## Why These Numbers Happened

**+20.6% calls answered:** Ring groups. With landlines, a call rang one phone. If that person was away, it went to voicemail. With VoIP ring groups, calls ring 4 agents simultaneously. Someone always picks up.

**-13.5% handle time:** Screen pop. When a call comes in, the CRM record appears automatically. No more "Can you spell your last name? What is your policy number?" The agent already has the information.

**-63.4% missed calls:** Same ring group effect, plus the mobile app. Agents who stepped away from their desk still received calls on their phone.

**-84.7% callback time:** Missed call notifications via email and app. Plus click-to-call — tap the notification, call connects immediately. No dialing, no looking up the number.

**+141.7% CRM entries:** Zero-effort logging. The integration logs every call automatically. The agent's job went from "make the call AND log the call" to just "make the call."

## The Dollar Value

| Improvement | Calculation | Annual Value |
|------------|-------------|-------------|
| +20.6% more calls answered | 7 more calls/agent/day x 65 agents x 250 days x $45 avg policy value | $511,875 |
| -84.7% faster callbacks | Reduced customer churn by ~2% x $1.2M annual premium | $24,000 |
| -13.5% handle time | 34 seconds saved x 41 calls x 65 agents x 250 days / 3600 = 5,133 hours x $28/hr | $143,733 |
| CRM documentation | Compliance audit cost avoidance | $50,000 |
| **Total productivity gain** | | **$729,608/year** |

The VoIP system costs $23,400/year (65 users x $30/month). The productivity gain is 31x the cost.

## The Caveat

These results are specific to an insurance brokerage with high call volume. A software company with 10 calls per day would see smaller absolute gains. But the percentage improvements — fewer missed calls, faster callbacks, automatic CRM logging — apply universally.

[DialPhone](https://dialphone.com) provides the CRM integration and call analytics that made these measurements possible. Their built-in reporting dashboard tracks all of these metrics without additional software.
